Mechanic Inventor opportunity: a partition-by-N tool (drag T objects into N labeled containers; system enforces equality; final per-container count IS the quotient) — currently only share-the-pizza covers partitive division explicitly.
3.OA.A.2.notes: share-the-pizza is partitive-only by design. bar-model is the only flexible engine covering both interpretations as-is.

3.OA.A.3.standardText: Use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
3.OA.A.3.namedSkill: Apply multiplication AND division within 100 to word problems across three named contexts: equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities. Drawings/equations used; unknown represented by a symbol.

primary.rationale: Singapore CPA word-problem canonical across mult and div, equal-groups and measurement contexts; supports unknown-symbol placement.
Equal-groups word-problem fit (boxes of items, batches of products); both factors visible; supports mult problems.
Measurement-quantity context (skip-count along a chain = distance); fits 'measurement quantities' clause directly.
Array context literally — the grid IS the array; fits 'arrays' clause directly.
Division word-problem fit; equal-groups context.
Area-as-measurement-quantity (W×H); fits 'measurement quantities' clause.
Measurement quantity (ingredient amounts × N) — measurement word-problem context.

secondary.rationale: Equal-groups visible but word-problem framing weak (game is operation-focused, not story-focused).
Same — equal-groups via replication, but the story framing is generic.
Multiplication operation; story affordance weaker than bar-model.
Iterated multiplication; the path-fork frame is decorative for word problems.

3.OA.A.3.notes: Strong coverage. 3.OA.A.3 is the broadest 3.OA standard; many engines map cleanly because the standard explicitly names three contexts (groups/arrays/measurement).

3.OA.A.4.standardText: Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation relating three whole numbers. For example, determine the unknown number that makes the equation true in each of the equations 8 × ? = 48, 5 = ? ÷ 3, 6 × 6 = ?.
3.OA.A.4.namedSkill: Find the missing whole number in equations of the form a × ? = c, ? × b = c, a × b = ?, a = ? ÷ b, ? = a ÷ b. Unknown-factor and unknown-quotient reasoning.

primary.rationale: Singapore CPA missing-piece bar — kid builds the part-whole picture with one section unknown; the rod-fit reveals the missing factor.

Beam pre-filled with mystery weight; kid adds weights summing to the mystery. The unknown-addend pattern extends cleanly to unknown-factor (group of weights summing to known total ÷ known factor). Within whole numbers.

secondary.rationale: True algebra (x + p = q via removal); structurally identifies an unknown but the 'remove from both sides' move is 6.EE, not 3.OA. Useful as extension.
Same algebra-structure concern as mystery-side.

revisable.fix: Lock product (target) and one slider (e.g., pallets = 8); kid must adjust the other slider until total matches — converts to unknown-factor solver ('8 × ? = 48').
Mark target flag at known product; lock skip-count value; kid slides marker until target reached, discovering the number of groups — unknown-factor reasoning.
Pre-set columns factor, give product target; kid fills until product reached; rows-filled is the unknown factor.
Pre-set base and target line; kid adjusts the multiplier — finds the unknown multiplier.

3.OA.A.4.coverageGap: Only 2 PRIMARY. Most candidates are REVISABLE because their default framing is build-the-product (3.OA.A.1 mode). Mechanic Inventor opportunity: a 'fact-family triangle' engine where kid drags any 2 of {a, b, c} into the triangle and the third auto-fits — explicitly serves 3.OA.A.4 + 3.OA.B.6.
3.OA.A.4.notes: If the REVISABLE engines get the unknown-factor mode, this standard moves to strong coverage. Priority fix list for the build queue.

K.CC.A.1.standardText: Count to 100 by ones and by tens.
K.CC.A.1.namedSkill: Recite the count sequence to 100, by 1s and by 10s. Production of the sequence, not interpretation.

primary.rationale: PRIMARY conditional on Audit 11 fix (forward-counting-trail mechanic where kid taps cells in order 1, 2, 3...). The 100-chart IS the visible representation of the standard's verbatim text 'Count to 100 by ones'; tapping cells in sequence enacts the production skill. Critic Criterion 4 (Construct Validity): the win condition becomes 'tap cells in sequence' — i.e., production-of-the-count — not pattern-matching. Skip-by-10s mode also serves the 'by tens' half of the standard. NOT PRIMARY until the REVISABLE fix ships.

Adapted as a count-the-bubbles forward-utterance engine: machine emits a starting number; child drags successive tiles in order; chime only when full forward run is correct. Critic Criterion 4: requires production of the next number names in stable order — the standard's named skill ('Count to 100 by ones'). Caveat: the engine's default framing is hidden-rule discovery (3.OA territory); using it for K.CC.A.1 requires re-skinning, which is a Builder-side decision in scenario picker, not an engine change.

Same adaptation as `sequence-builder`: a 'count-by-tens' rule machine where the child experiments with starting values and observes the +10 pattern, then locks in the rule. Serves the 'by tens' half of the standard verbatim text. Critic Criterion 4: the win condition is recognizing the +10 successor pattern across the decade words 10, 20, 30..., which is exactly what the NBT Progression p. 55 calls out as the pedagogical purpose of counting by tens at K.

secondary.rationale: Skip-counting by N on a chain is structurally related; but bead-chain is 3.OA framed and exceeds K range.

revisable.fix: Currently click-correct-cell (binary); revise to a forward-counting-trail mechanic where kid taps cells in order 1, 2, 3... and the trail glows. Then becomes a count-sequence enforcer.

K.CC.A.1.coverageGap: Conditional coverage. 3 PRIMARY engines proposed but all three carry caveats — `hundred-board` requires its REVISABLE fix to ship, and `sequence-builder`/`pattern-machine` require Builder-side scenario re-skinning for K.CC.A.1 (they were designed for hidden-rule-discovery in higher grades). Audit 13 §4 Mechanic Inventor opportunity — a voice-or-tap-in-sequence count-the-bubbles engine — remains the right long-term fix.
K.CC.A.1.notes: K.CC.A.1 is a production skill — recite — which is unusual for screen-game mechanics. Voice/tap-in-sequence engine is the right shape but doesn't exist.

K.CC.A.2.standardText: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence (instead of having to begin at 1).
K.CC.A.2.namedSkill: Counting-on from a non-1 start. The kid must NOT recount from 1.

primary.rationale: PRIMARY conditional on the existing REVISABLE fix already listed in this standard's entry (add 'counting-on' mode: pre-fill first frame with N counters, child taps additional counters and count starts at N+1). With the fix, this directly enacts the standard's verbatim text 'Count forward beginning from a given number ... instead of having to begin at 1.' Critic Criterion 4: win condition is taps-after-N, not taps-from-1; structurally enforces counting-on. Move from REVISABLE to PRIMARY once shipped.

PRIMARY conditional on the K.CC.A.1 trail fix plus a 'start-from-N' variant. With the kid placed at cell N and asked to tap forward through N+1, N+2..., the engine directly serves K.CC.A.2's verbatim 'count forward beginning from a given number.' Critic Criterion 4: the win condition cannot be satisfied by recounting from 1 — the starting cell is fixed at N.

Adapted as 'pre-stocked field' mode: the field starts with N dots already present (a given starting cardinality); child taps additional clusters and the running count begins at N+1, not 1. Critic Criterion 4: the kid's taps produce a count that begins from N — exactly the standard's verbatim 'count forward beginning from a given number.' Cardinality-commit at end matches Chesure's K.OA cardinality-commit pattern.

revisable.fix: Add 'counting-on' mode: pre-fill first frame with N counters as a given start, child taps additional counters and the count starts at N+1, not 1. Currently the kid counts all from 1.

K.CC.A.2.coverageGap: Conditional coverage. 3 PRIMARY engines proposed but all three require feature additions: `number-frames` needs the existing REVISABLE 'counting-on' fix; `hundred-board` needs the K.CC.A.1 trail fix plus a start-from-N variant; `free-collect` needs a pre-stocked-field mode. The Mechanic Inventor opportunity remains the cleanest path. Critical foundational gap for 1.OA strategies.

K.CC.A.3.standardText: Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0-20 (with 0 representing a count of no objects).
K.CC.A.3.namedSkill: Produce the numeral form (write or select) for a given quantity 0–20. Writing is a fine-motor production skill; pairing-numeral-with-quantity is the screen-friendly half.

primary.rationale: Quantity-numeral pairing (Part B only): child fills the ten-frame to a target count and commits the matching numeral on a number-pad at end. Critic Criterion 4: the cardinality-commit IS the numeral-quantity pairing — child cannot commit until the frame matches the numeral. Range 0–20 (two ten-frames) covers the standard's full range. Handwriting (Part A) NOT covered — see scoping note.

Quantity-numeral pairing (Part B only): a numeral 0–20 is shown as the target; child taps to collect that many dots into the field. The field-size IS the cardinality, paired with the numeral target. Critic Criterion 4: win condition requires the field to match the numeral — direct numeral-to-quantity mapping. Includes the 0-case (target = 0, no taps) which the standard's verbatim text explicitly names ('0 representing a count of no objects'). Handwriting (Part A) NOT covered.

Quantity-numeral pairing (Part B only): a numeral 0–20 is shown; child snaps rods until the train-length matches the numeral. The rod length encodes the cardinality (Chesure §2.1 'length IS the count' for unit rods). Critic Criterion 4: numeral on screen, rod-length on table; the kid IS pairing them. Range 0–20 works with 10-rods + unit rods (the 10-rod itself is the standard's 'first use of 0 as placeholder' anchor named in the Progressions excerpt). Handwriting (Part A) NOT covered.

secondary.rationale: Quantity is shown; numeral is committed at the end as a tap-on-numeral; partially covers the numeral-quantity pairing but the writing/production aspect is absent.

K.CC.A.3.coverageGap: Partial coverage: 3 PRIMARY engines now serve the numeral-quantity pairing half of the standard, but the numeral-WRITING half (fine-motor stroke production) has no engine in the current library. A trace-the-numeral engine with stroke-path validation remains the Mechanic Inventor opportunity from Audit 13 §4.
K.CC.A.3.notes: Production-of-numeral has no precedent in the current registry — every engine pre-renders numerals.

K.CC.B.4.standardText: Understand the relationship between numbers and quantities; connect counting to cardinality.
K.CC.B.4.namedSkill: Cardinality — the last number counted tells how many. Encompasses K.CC.B.4a, 4b, 4c. Foundational pre-K.OA skill.

primary.rationale: Tap to count each cluster's dots into the field; the field-size IS the cardinality of taps; no running count digit (Chesure §3.6 enforces one-to-one and cardinality).
Filling the ten-frame is one-to-one (each tap = one counter); cardinality emerges as the final count.
Length = count when units are the smallest rod; the rod's length IS the cardinality.
Each rod is one unit; the rod-train length IS the cardinality.

secondary.rationale: Filling cells one-by-one teaches one-to-one and cardinality (each placed tile = +1 to area).

revisable.fix: Per Audit 11 §2 fix #4: hide target picture, show count-words only ('2 triangles, 1 square'); kid must count what they place. Then becomes a strong cardinality engine.

K.CC.B.4.notes: K.CC.B.4 is well-served because every K.OA engine inherits cardinality enforcement.

K.CC.B.4a.standardText: When counting objects, say the number names in the standard order, pairing each object with one and only one number name and each number name with one and only one object.
K.CC.B.4a.namedSkill: One-to-one correspondence in counting. Each object counted exactly once.

primary.rationale: Each tap moves exactly one set into the field — one-to-one structurally enforced; no double-counting possible.
Each ten-frame cell holds one counter; one-to-one is geometric.
Each rod-unit is discrete; one-to-one is structural.

secondary.rationale: Per-unit rods; one-to-one enforced.

K.CC.B.4a.notes: Inherited from K.CC.B.4. All count-and-collect engines pass.

K.CC.B.4b.standardText: Understand that the last number name said tells the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of their arrangement or the order in which they were counted.
K.CC.B.4b.namedSkill: Cardinality (last-count rule) AND order-irrelevance (same total across arrangements). Order-irrelevance is the key construct here.

primary.rationale: Clusters can be tapped in any order; the field reaches the same total — order-irrelevance is structural. Cardinality enforced by snap-when-equal.

Cardinality enforced (rod length IS the count) AND order-irrelevance is structural — the same target-length train can be assembled by snapping rods in any order (5 = 2+3 = 3+2 = 1+4 = 4+1 in rod-order; the final length is invariant). Critic Criterion 4: the standard's verbatim text 'the number of objects is the same regardless of ... the order in which they were counted' is enacted by the engine — child can build the same total train rod-order-agnostically and verify the length is unchanged.

Cardinality enforced (unit-rod count = bar length) AND order-irrelevance demonstrated by building the same bar via different unit-rod sequences. Critic Criterion 4: the standard's 'same regardless of arrangement or order' clause is supported by side-by-side bars built in different orders that visibly match in length. Currently SECONDARY for K.CC.B.4b but the engine's structural support for order-irrelevance promotes it to PRIMARY when the Builder uses a 'same total, different build order' scenario.

secondary.rationale: Cardinality enforced; but Chesure §3.6 notes 'does not vary the starting position to enforce order-irrelevance.' Partial.
Cardinality via length; order-irrelevance demonstrable when same train built from different rod orders.
Same as cuisenaire-rods.

K.CC.B.4b.notes: Order-irrelevance is the named-skill nuance; free-collect is the strongest demonstrator because its clusters can be tapped in any order.

K.CC.B.4c.standardText: Understand that each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger.
K.CC.B.4c.namedSkill: The 'one more' principle — successor function. n+1 reasoning.

primary.rationale: Each counter added is visibly 'one more' than the prior state; ten-frame structure makes successive +1 immediate.
Tapping a single-dot cluster adds exactly one — 'one more' enacted.

Swap a unit rod onto the end of a train to enact the +1 successor; the new train-length is visibly 'one larger' than the previous. Critic Criterion 4: the standard's verbatim text 'each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger' is enacted physically — the child sees and feels the single-unit increment. Currently SECONDARY (the listed rationale says 'available but not the engine's primary action') but when the Builder picks a 'add one more' scenario (e.g., 'one more cookie on the plate'), the +1 increment becomes the engine's primary action, qualifying it as PRIMARY for this standard.

secondary.rationale: Swap a rod for one 1-unit longer to see 'one more'; available but not the engine's primary action.
Same as cuisenaire-rods.

K.CC.B.5.standardText: Count to answer 'how many?' questions about as many as 20 things arranged in a line, a rectangular array, or a circle, or as many as 10 things in a scattered configuration; given a number from 1–20, count out that many objects.
K.CC.B.5.namedSkill: Count up to 20 across arrangements (line/array/circle/scatter); also count-out-N. Range is 1–20 (above K.OA's 10 ceiling).

primary.rationale: Count-out-N (target field of dots) is literally the win condition; arrangement-agnostic clusters.
Line/array arrangement (2×5 frame); count-out-N via taps.
Count-out-N as length-build to target.

secondary.rationale: Count-out-N via rod-train; line arrangement only.
Rectangular-array arrangement; count to fill.

revisable.fix: Hide target picture, show count-words; kid counts out N shapes (Audit 11 §2 fix #4).

K.CC.B.5.notes: Scattered-configuration coverage is weakest; most engines structure objects into frames/lines.

K.CC.C.6.standardText: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group, e.g., by using matching and counting strategies (include groups with up to ten objects).
K.CC.C.6.namedSkill: Compare two object-groups by greater/less/equal using matching or counting. Groups up to 10. Objects (not numerals — that is K.CC.C.7).

primary.rationale: Block widths show value; physics validates >/</= ordering. SRT via wobble-and-slide enforces compare.
Height-blocks; reorder into descending staircase — direct compare-by-height physical model.
Length-compare; longer rod IS greater. Concrete model for compare-by-matching.
Side-by-side bar comparison; matching strategy via bar-end-alignment.

secondary.rationale: Two-target compare possible but engine is single-target by default.

K.CC.C.6.notes: Strong coverage for the object-group form.

K.CC.C.7.standardText: Compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals.
K.CC.C.7.namedSkill: Compare TWO WRITTEN NUMERALS, 1–10. Numeral-to-numeral compare. No objects required.

primary.rationale: Numerals shown as weight-blocks (width encodes value); compare via physics. Works because numerals are visible alongside the physical encoding.
Same — numerals on height-blocks; compare-by-height.

Two numerals presented; child places them on a number line and the relative position IS the compare-result. Critic Criterion 4: the standard's verbatim text 'compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals' is enacted — the kid places the numerals and reads off greater/less by physical position. PRIMARY-as-is for the simple K numeral-compare context (the Audit 11 REVISABLE flag for 'tighter snap + numerical readout' applies to higher-grade decimal/fraction use, not to integer 1–10 compare). Same context-dependent-verdict pattern Audit 13 §5 applied to `size-picker`.

Two numerals shown; child builds a bar for each and aligns the ends — longer bar = greater numeral. Critic Criterion 4: the comparison is by physical bar-length, with the numerals visible alongside; the kid IS comparing the numerals via the bar-length encoding. Currently PRIMARY for K.CC.C.6 (object-group compare); promotion to PRIMARY for K.CC.C.7 follows because the engine accepts numeral input directly, not just object groups.

secondary.rationale: Compare-two-items; Audit 11 marks REVISABLE because unit-mismatch is needed at higher grades, but for K simple numeral compare it's adequate. Risk: Shortcut Adversary — kid reads digits without compare-reasoning.

revisable.fix: For K.CC.C.7 specifically, no fix needed — the engine's 'pick the bigger' with two visible numerals matches the standard exactly. The Audit 11 REVISABLE flag is for higher-grade unit-mismatch use.

K.CC.C.7.notes: size-picker double-listed (secondary AND revisable) reflects context-dependent verdict: PRIMARY-as-is for K.CC.C.7 simple numeral-compare; REVISABLE for measurement-compare grades.
